segments.text: Scientists think that a whopping 75% of
life in our oceans glows. And the most
infamous one,
>> it's so pretty,
>> isn't even doing it right. This lure is
actually glowy bacteria. Researchers
think that young angler fish draw them
in from the seawater, but other animals,
much bigger than bacteria, can glow all
on their own. Most of them use a
chemical reaction between two molecules
and oxygen. This mix triggers a reaction
that creates light. But the exact
chemicals vary animal to animal. Meaning
this ability to glow evolved tons of
different times independently. Over 25
times in fish alone. So why can't I
glow? And they all use it for different
reasons like predation and defense and
even a sort of alarm. We're still
figuring out all the ways light is used
in the darkest parts of our oceans. But
learning how could be key to helping us
uncover the beautiful monsters in the
deep. To learn more about them,
subscribe.
